pick.rationale: Florida enters as the top-5 overall team with elite efficiency on both ends, ranking 4th in KenPom and Torvik, while Iowa's 25th-26th ranking shows a clear talent gap. The Gators' top-5 defense should disrupt Iowa's potent offense, and Florida's recent form—including a 26-7 record and dominant first-round win—provides momentum. Iowa's path relies on hot three-point shooting and forcing turnovers, but Florida's superior efficiency, depth, and matchup advantage make them the reliable pick to advance.

reasoningStep.title: Team Quality and Efficiency Analysis
reasoningStep.summary: Florida holds a decisive edge in advanced metrics, ranking 4th nationally in both KenPom and Torvik, compared to Iowa's 25th-26th ranking. The Gators boast a top-5 defense and top-11 offense, while Iowa's defense is weaker (35th in Torvik), creating a clear matchup advantage for Florida.
reasoningStep.evidence: Florida: KenPom rank 4, Torvik rank 4, adjO 125.6 (9th), adjD 91.8 (5th). Iowa: KenPom rank 25, Torvik rank 26, adjO 121.8 (31st), adjD 99.4 (35th).

toolCalls.summary: Returned combined Torvik/KenPom ratings for 2 teams.

toolCalls.summary: Fetched https://hawkeyeswire.usatoday.com/story/sports/college/hawkeyes/mens-basketball/2026/03/17/iowa-basketball-march-madness-florida-upset/89189536007/ and extracted 2904 characters of text.

result.text: Analyst scoffs at idea Iowa basketball could knock off Florida Skip to main content Home Schedule Football Basketball Women's Basketball Recruiting Big Ten Sports Odds Newsletter College Sports Wire USA TODAY Sports Analyst scoffs at idea Iowa basketball could knock off Florida Zach Hiney Hawkeyes Wire March is the time for madness in the world of college basketball. March Madness isn't just some corporate catchphrase. It's an annual reality and why so many people love the NCAA Tournament. The idea that any team is capable of beating another on a given day. It doesn't matter what the seeds are or what the trends dictate. Every team in the dance is 40 minutes away from the win of a lifetime. That includes the Iowa basketball team. While they limped into the tournament, winning just one of their final five games to end the regular season, this is a new season now. The Hawkeyes have played some really good basketball this season, taking numerous fantastic teams down to the wire. They're not far away from some of the elite teams this sport has to offer. Iowa got a tough draw , facing off with a good Clemson team in the first round. If they survive the Tigers, they'll most likely get Florida , the No. 1 seed in the South Region. The Gators are an outstanding team that would be favored in a hypothetical matchup with the Hawkeyes. But this is March, and anything can happen. Don't tell that to NBC college basketball analyst John Fanta, though. Fanta was a guest on FS1's morning show "Wake Up Barstool" on Monday, where he confronted one of the hosts, Liam Blutman, who said that Iowa would have a chance to beat Florida in a potential Round of 32 matchup. "If you think Iowa is going to have a real opportunity against Florida, child please," Fanta mockingly exclaimed. While Iowa needs to worry about Clemson first and foremost, Ben McCollum should keep this clip on standby should his team advance in the tournament. Iowa would already have a ton of motivation to win the game, but this could provide the extra bulletin board material they need to really compete in the game. Should Iowa be the betting favorite to make the Sweet 16? No. But to just completely rule out the possibility of them making a run seems very naive and shortsighted for someone with the basketball knowledge that Fanta possesses. He should know better than most that anything is possible in March. Iowa's pursuit to prove Fanta wrong will begin on Friday at 5:50 p.m. CT when they take on Clemson. The game can be seen on TNT. Contact/Follow us @HawkeyesWire on X (formerly Twitter) and like our page on Facebook to follow ongoing coverage of Iowa news, notes and opinions. result.text: 2026 NCAA Men's Basketball Tournament Breakdown | FOX Sports my favs Access and manage your favorites here DISMISS Scores Watch Betting WBC ⚾ FOX Super 6 💰 Stories Search Sign In Account SPORTS & TEAMS PLAYERS SHOWS PERSONALITIES TOPICS POPULAR SEARCHES - No Data Available - BROWSE BY SPORTS & TEAMS PLAYERS SHOWS PERSONALITIES TOPICS Built on College Basketball College Basketball NCAA Men's Basketball Tournament Breakdown: Top Matchups, Upsets, Predictions Updated Mar. 15, 2026 11:03 p.m. ET share facebook x reddit link Michael Cohen College Football and College Basketball Writer At last, the madness is upon us. Five long months of buildup to Selection Sunday came to an end with another glorious reveal of this year’s NCAA Men's Basketball Tournament bracket. There are heavyweights at the top in 1-seeds Duke , Arizona , Michigan and Florida . There are newcomers like Queens and California Baptist. And there are more enticing storylines than fans can consume: from Miami (OH) in the First Four to potential No. 1 overall draft picks in BYU ’s AJ Dybantsa and Kansas’ Darryn Peterson, from Nebraska seeking the first March Madness win in program history to the all-important health of Duke’s two injured starters. Without further ado, let’s break down the field: [MARCH MADNESS: NCAA Men's Basketball Tournament Bracket ] 1. Of the No. 1 seeds, which team has the clearest path to the Final Four? Koa Peat #10 of the Arizona Wildcats reacts in front of Allen Mukeba #23 of the Arizona State Sun Devils. (Photo by Chris Coduto/Getty Images) Arizona. Given the legitimate injury questions surrounding Duke, a string of sluggish performances from Michigan in the Big Ten Tournament and a blowout loss by Florida in the SEC Tournament, there’s no question that Arizona enters the Big Dance with the most encouraging trajectory of the top seeds. The Wildcats are one of only two teams to rank among the top five nationally in both offensive and defensive efficiency at this point, alongside Duke, and they’ve won nine consecutive games ahead of a first-round matchup with No. 16 LIU. A roster littered with future NBA Draft picks, including two likely lottery picks in shooting guard Brayden Burries and power forward Koa Peat, gives head coach Tommy Lloyd his best chance of advancing beyond the Sweet 16 since taking over at Arizona. The Wildcats' potential path to the Final Four seems relatively friendly, too. Second-seeded Purdue only rose to the 2-line thanks to an upset over Michigan in the Big Ten title game, which means the Boilermakers’ overall profile is more in line with that of a 3-seed. Third-seeded Gonzaga , while talented, is not believed to be on the same level of classic Bulldogs teams under head coach Mark Few. Arkansas and Wisconsin, seeded fourth and fifth, respectively, also needed strong runs in their respective conference tournaments to land on those lines. The West feels like a region with a handful of overseeded teams trying to take down arguably

modelTrace.content:
You are an analyst predicting a single March Madness game inside a bracket run.
You have live tools. Use them before making a pick when they can reduce uncertainty.
You are not filling the whole bracket in one response. Predict only the current game.
Use prior picks as already committed bracket state.
You may use at most 10 tool rounds before you must finalize your answer.
Before making a pick, investigate relevant context such as injuries or availability concerns, recent team news, prior head-to-head results when useful, and multiple raw statistics from the available ratings and matchup data.
Take a holistic approach. Balance team quality, matchup specifics, health, form, schedule context, and upset risk instead of relying on one metric.
Do not force an upset, but explicitly consider whether the underdog has a credible path to win and let that affect confidence and rationale.
Return strict JSON only.
Final JSON keys: pick, reasoningStep.
pick must contain gameId, winnerId, confidence, rationale.
The rationale must be a short paragraph of 2 to 4 sentences explaining why the winner was chosen.
reasoningStep must contain id, title, summary, evidence.

Tournament year: 2026
Current game id: r32-south-1
Current game label: South Round of 32 1
Round: Round of 32

Prior committed picks: 44

Without further ado, let’s break down the field: [MARCH MADNESS: NCAA Men's Basketball Tournament Bracket ] 1. Of the No. 1 seeds, which team has the clearest path to the Final Four? Koa Peat #10 of the Arizona Wildcats reacts in front of Allen Mukeba #23 of the Arizona State Sun Devils. (Photo by Chris Coduto/Getty Images) Arizona. Given the legitimate injury questions surrounding Duke, a string of sluggish performances from Michigan in the Big Ten Tournament and a blowout loss by Florida in the SEC Tournament, there’s no question that Arizona enters the Big Dance with the most encouraging trajectory of the top seeds. The Wildcats are one of only two teams to rank among the top five nationally in both offensive and defensive efficiency at this point, alongside Duke, and they’ve won nine consecutive games ahead of a first-round matchup with No. 16 LIU. A roster littered with future NBA Draft picks, including two likely lottery picks in shooting guard Brayden Burries and power forward Koa Peat, gives head coach Tommy Lloyd his best chance of advancing beyond the Sweet 16 since taking over at Arizona. The Wildcats' potential path to the Final Four seems relatively friendly, too. Second-seeded Purdue only rose to the 2-line thanks to an upset over Michigan in the Big Ten title game, which means the Boilermakers’ overall profile is more in line with that of a 3-seed. Third-seeded Gonzaga , while talented, is not believed to be on the same level of classic Bulldogs teams under head coach Mark Few. Arkansas and Wisconsin, seeded fourth and fifth, respectively, also needed strong runs in their respective conference tournaments to land on those lines. The West feels like a region with a handful of overseeded teams trying to take down arguably
